pattern: Doubling odd values first turns the problem into repeatedly halving the current maximum (only while it stays even), so a max-heap greedily narrows the max-min gap toward its true optimum.
hints: Every odd value can only ever grow (by doubling once) before it behaves like an even value; do that doubling up front so every number is even to start.
The maximum can always be shrunk by halving it as long as it stays even, and shrinking the maximum can only help; halving the minimum would only hurt, so never do that.
Keep all values in a max-heap plus track the running minimum. Repeatedly: record max-min, then if the max is even, pop it, halve it, update the minimum, and push it back; stop once the max is odd.

edge_inputs.description: small mix of odd and even values
already-equal deviation of a pair after one halving
all values already odd, each doubles exactly once
a value with a long chain of factors of two, tempting an early stop
one large power-of-two value dominates and must be halved all the way down
every value already even and equal: deviation is zero from the start

stress.description: large random array of mixed parity values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

solutions.python:
import heapq
class Solution:
    def minimumDeviation(self, nums):
        heap = []
        lo = float('inf')
        for x in nums:
            if x % 2 == 1:
                x *= 2
            heap.append(-x)
            lo = min(lo, x)
        heapq.heapify(heap)
        best = -heap[0] - lo
        while (-heap[0]) % 2 == 0:
            x = -heapq.heappop(heap)
            x //= 2
            lo = min(lo, x)
            heapq.heappush(heap, -x)
            best = min(best, -heap[0] - lo)
        return best
